Other filing statuses (single, HOH, MFS) NOT explicitly reset in OBBBA text - practitioner commentary identifies as potential drafting oversight pending technical correction. 2025 exemptions: $88,100 single, $137,000 MFJ, $68,500 MFS, $29,900 estates/trusts. AMT rates: 26% on first $232,600 of AMT base (2025); 28% on excess. Key add-backs under §56(b): SALT (§56(b)(1)(A)(ii)) - 100% disallowed for AMT; with OBBBA $40K SALT cap, larger add-back exposure; standard deduction (§56(b)(1)(E)); ISO bargain element preference (§56(b)(3)); accelerated depreciation difference (§56(a)(1)); certain private activity bond interest (§57(a)(5)). §168(k) bonus depreciation allowed for AMT (no add-back). §199A QBI deduction allowed for AMT. LTCG and qualified dividends at preferential rates for AMT under §55(b)(3). ISO bargain element major tech-employee trigger - exercise creates AMT preference even without regular tax. §53 Minimum Tax Credit recovers AMT paid on TIMING items only (ISO, depreciation, NOL) - NOT exclusion items (SALT, standard deduction); Form 8801; indefinite carryforward; lost at death. State AMTs in CA, IA, MN. Form 6251 mechanics.
- form-3520-foreign-gifts-trusts.html: Form 3520 (Annual Return to Report Transactions With Foreign Trusts and Receipt of Certain Foreign Gifts) complete mechanics. IRC §6048 foreign trust transactions; §6039F foreign gift reporting. Four trigger types: (1) creation/transfer to foreign trust (Part I, 35% penalty); (2) US owner of foreign grantor trust §§671-679 (Part II, 5% penalty plus Form 3520-A by trust); (3) distribution from foreign trust ANY amount (Part III, 35% penalty under §6677); (4) foreign gifts above threshold (Part IV, 25% max penalty under §6039F). Thresholds 2025/2026: $100,000 aggregate from NRA or foreign estate; $20,116 (2025) / $20,573 (2026) inflation-indexed from foreign corp/partnership; ANY amount foreign trust distribution. Aggregation across related foreign donors (parents combined). Filed SEPARATELY from Form 1040 - mailed to Ogden UT (most common error: filing with 1040). Rev. Proc. 2014-55 exempts Canadian RRSP/RRIF/RPP/PRPP/SPP. Rev. Proc. 2020-17 exempts certain tax-favored foreign retirement/savings trusts. Reg §301.7701-7 court and control tests define foreign trust. Throwback tax §665-668 on accumulation distributions with compound interest charge §668(a); capital gain character LOST under §667(a). §6662(j) 40% accuracy penalty for undisclosed foreign trust assets. Reasonable cause defense §6677(d). Proposed May 2024 regs would reduce $100K threshold to inflation-indexed ($20K) for all foreign donors; election to rely if applied consistently. Streamlined Filing Compliance Procedures and Delinquent IIR Submission Procedures provide relief for non-willful and reasonable-cause failures. §2503(e) excludes direct-to-provider tuition and medical payments. §2801 succession tax (40%) on gifts from covered expatriates under §877A - separate from Form 3520 reporting (January 2025 final regs). Farhy theories applicable to §6038 not §6677. Form 3520-A due 15th day 3rd month after trust year-end; substitute Form 3520-A by US owner if trust fails to file.
- form-1099-da-crypto-broker-2026.html: Form 1099-DA (Digital Asset Proceeds From Broker Transactions) - first IRS form specifically for digital asset reporting. IRC §6045 modified by Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act (P.L. 117-58, November 2021). T.D. 10000 (June 28, 2024) final custodial broker regulations. T.D. 10021 (December 27, 2024) DeFi broker regulations VACATED by Congressional Review Act resolution March 2025; only custodial brokers remain in scope. Phase-in: 2025 transactions gross proceeds only; 2026 transactions add basis for covered digital assets (acquired after 12/31/2025 in custodial account and held there); 2027 backup withholding §3406 begins in earnest; real estate professionals report FMV in real estate transactions paid with crypto. Custodial brokers include: centralized exchanges, custodial trading platforms, hosted wallet providers, digital asset kiosks (crypto ATMs), PDAPs (payment processors); NFT marketplaces if custodial. Rev. Proc. 2024-28 (June 2024) ELIMINATED universal accounting; mandatory wallet-by-wallet basis allocation effective January 1, 2025; safe harbor allocation methods (specific identification or global allocation by reasonable methodology). Treas. Reg. §1.1012-1(j) digital asset basis rules. Notice 2024-56 penalty relief for good-faith broker reporting in 2025; backup withholding relief through 2026 generally and through 2027 for TIN-matched accounts (Notice 2025-33). Notice 2024-57 identifies certain deferred transactions including notional principal contracts. Qualifying stablecoin de minimis under Reg §1.6045-1(c)(13)(iv) - aggregate reporting permitted. Specified NFT special rules - $600 threshold, aggregate reporting, Box 11c for minter sales. Dual classification assets (tokenized securities) move from Form 1099-B to Form 1099-DA. Customer-provided basis (Box 8) for lot ordering only - cannot be reported as basis to IRS. Form 8949 boxes: A/D covered with basis; B/E noncovered; C/F not on 1099 at all (non-custodial transactions still taxable). Wash sale §1091 generally NOT applicable to digital assets (not stock or securities). Mining/staking rewards as ordinary income at FMV per Notice 2014-21 and Rev. Rul. 2023-14, not on 1099-DA.
- section-461l-excess-business-loss-permanent.html: IRC §461(l) excess business loss limitation. OBBBA P.L. 119-21 made §461(l) PERMANENT (removed 2028 sunset) effective tax years beginning after 12/31/2025. Inflation indexing base year RESET from 2017 to 2024 - approximately 18% reduction in 2026 thresholds vs 2025. 2025 thresholds (final TCJA-path year): $313,000 single / $626,000 MFJ. 2026 thresholds (OBBBA reset): ~$256,000 single / ~$512,000 MFJ. MFS half. Trusts/estates at single amount. Mechanism: aggregate ALL trade or business deductions vs aggregate ALL business income/gains; if net loss exceeds threshold, excess is EBL disallowed for current year and carried forward as §172 NOL subject to 80% of taxable income limit. Order of limitations (must clear in sequence): (1) basis §704(d)/§1366(d); (2) at-risk §465; (3) passive activity §469; (4) excess business loss §461(l) LAST. W-2 wages NOT business income for §461(l) - cannot offset beyond threshold. §1231 gains EXPAND the cap dollar-for-dollar (planning lever for asset sales in loss years). Capital losses NOT included (separate §1211 $3,000 net cap limit). NOL deduction (§172), §199A QBI deduction excluded from §461(l) computation. OBBBA codified individual-level aggregation across ALL pass-through entities owned by taxpayer; spouses combine on joint return; one spouse's profitable business offsets other spouse's losses before §461(l) limit. OBBBA confirmed NOL converted from EBL is NOT retested under §461(l) in future years (rejecting alternative bill version). Form 461 required if business losses exceed threshold; attached to Form 1040/1040-NR/1041/1041-QFT/1041-N/990-T. Equipment leasing tax shelter promises often misleadingly omit §461(l) - bonus depreciation under §168(k) restored to 100% under OBBBA generates large first-year deductions but §461(l) caps offset of non-business income. California and some states do not conform - state losses may be available without limit.
- pte-passthrough-entity-tax-state-workaround.html: Pass-Through Entity Tax (PTET) state-level workaround to federal SALT cap §164(b)(6). IRS Notice 2020-75 (November 9, 2020) federal blessing - state income tax paid by partnership or S-corporation at entity level deductible under §162 in computing non-separately stated income or loss. Connecticut enacted first PTET 2018; 36+ states plus New York City now have regimes. OBBBA P.L. 119-21 PRESERVED PTET despite raising federal SALT cap to $40,000 for 2025-2029 (House version threatened limits, rejected in final law). PTET still valuable post-OBBBA because $40K cap phases down to $10K floor above $500K MAGI (30% phaseout rate); above $600K MAGI cap is $10K floor only; PTET bypasses individual cap entirely. State election mechanics vary dramatically: New York annual irrevocable election by March 15 (pending Sept 15 proposal); 6.85%-10.9% graduated rate. NYC PTET layered on NYS, requires all-NYC-resident owners. California 9.3% rate extended through 2030; mandatory $1,000 or 50% prior year prepayment by June 15 (2025 binary, 2026-2030 has cure provision). Connecticut converted MANDATORY to ELECTIVE in 2024. Illinois made PERMANENT December 12, 2025. Michigan extended election to 9-month-after-year-end; 3-year binding election. Two PTET models: credit model (most states, owner takes nonrefundable credit) vs reduced income model (K-1 shows reduced income). §1361(b)(1)(D) S-corp single class of stock requires proportionate PTET allocations. Investment partnerships unclear under Notice 2020-75. Single-member LLC disregarded entity cannot elect PTET. Tiered partnerships create complexity. Federal interactions: PTET reduces business income affecting §199A QBI deduction, §163(j) ATI, §461(l) EBL computation, basis tracking. Also helps with AMT - SALT not deductible for AMT under §56(b)(1)(A)(ii). Tax distribution clauses in partnership/S-corp agreements need modification. Composite return is DIFFERENT from PTET. Resident state credit for PTET paid to another state varies.
- section-1031-like-kind-exchange-deep.html: IRC §1031 (Exchange of real property held for productive use or investment) complete mechanics. TCJA P.L. 115-97 §13303 (December 22, 2017) eliminated personal property and intangibles from §1031 - REAL PROPERTY ONLY effective January 1, 2018. OBBBA P.L. 119-21 (July 4, 2025) PRESERVED §1031 unchanged - rejected proposed $500,000 caps. New §1062 farmland deferral provision added by OBBBA for qualified farmland sales to qualified farmers (tax years beginning after July 4, 2025) - SEPARATE non-recognition provision. Eligible property: real property held for productive use in trade or business or for investment, located in United States (foreign real estate NOT like-kind under §1031(h)). Time deadlines (both hard - no extensions for weekends/holidays): 45-day identification (Day 45 midnight cutoff); 180-day exchange OR earlier of return due date with extensions. Q4 trap: relinquished property sold October 17, 2025 onward forces Form 4868 extension to preserve full 180 days. Three identification rules: three-property (any value); 200% rule (any number, total FMV not exceeding 200% of relinquished); 95% exception (rarely used). Qualified Intermediary (QI) under Reg §1.1031(k)-1(g)(4) safe harbor - taxpayer cannot have actual or constructive receipt; disqualified persons (agent within 2 years, related party §267(b)/§707(b)(1), employee). Five exchange structures: simultaneous, delayed (forward, most common), reverse (Rev. Proc. 2000-37 with Exchange Accommodation Titleholder parking), improvement/build-to-suit, combined reverse + improvement. Boot - cash, mortgage relief, non-like-kind property - triggers gain recognition up to lesser of realized gain or boot received. Character of recognized gain retained (capital gain, §1231, §1245/§1250 recapture). §1031(f) related-party 2-year holding requirement. 15% incidental personal property rule under Reg §1.1031(a)-3 (effective after December 2, 2020) - personal property typically transferred with real property NOT treated as separate if 15% or less of replacement real property FMV. §121 coordination per Rev. Proc. 2005-14 - §121 applied FIRST, then §1031. §1031 replacement converted to residence requires 5 years holding under §121(d)(10). California §18031.5 clawback - sources eventual gain to California even with non-CA replacement; annual FTB 3840 reporting. Pennsylvania does NOT conform. Form 8824 reporting required. Depreciation recapture deferred via §1031; preserved in replacement property basis; on ultimate sale, recapture extends across multiple exchanged properties.
- section-121-home-sale-exclusion-deep.html: IRC §121 (Exclusion of gain from sale of principal residence) complete mechanics. Taxpayer Relief Act of 1997, P.L. 105-34 enacted current framework. $250,000 single / head of household / married filing separately; $500,000 MFJ. NOT inflation-indexed since 1997 - amounts unchanged for nearly three decades. OBBBA did NOT modify exclusion limits. Eligibility: ownership test (owned 2 of last 5 years before sale) AND use test (used as principal residence 2 of last 5 years) - 730 days suffices, need not be continuous, need not coincide. §121(b)(3) frequency limitation - available no more than once every 2 years. §121(b)(4) surviving spouse - $500,000 cap if sale within 2 YEARS of deceased spouse's death AND requirements met immediately before death AND surviving spouse not remarried. §121(b)(5) nonqualified use (Housing Assistance Tax Act of 2008) - effective for periods of nonqualified use AFTER January 1, 2009. Allocation formula: nonqualified gain = total gain × (nonqualified periods / total ownership periods). Trailing period exception §121(b)(5)(C)(ii)(I) - period AFTER last principal residence use within 5-year window is NOT nonqualified use. Military 10-year exception §121(b)(5)(C)(ii)(II). Temporary absence 2-year exception §121(b)(5)(C)(ii)(III). §121(d)(6) depreciation recapture - depreciation after May 6, 1997 NEVER excludable; taxed as unrecaptured §1250 gain max 25% under §1(h)(1)(D); reported Form 4797. §121(c)(1) partial exclusion safe harbors when 2-of-5 tests fail: change of employment (50-mile farther test under Reg §1.121-3(c)); health (physician recommendation Reg §1.121-3(d)); unforeseen circumstances Reg §1.121-3(e) (death, divorce, multiple birth, unemployment, casualty, terrorism, change rendering home unaffordable). §121(d)(9) military qualified extended duty - up to 10-year suspension of 5-year clock. §121(d)(10) §1031 replacement - 5-year minimum holding before §121 available. NIIT §1411 - excluded gain NOT subject; non-excluded portion IS subject if MAGI thresholds exceeded. 2026 LTCG brackets per Rev. Proc. 2025-32: 0% up to $49,450 single / $98,900 MFJ; 15% to $545,500 / $613,700; 20% above. Joint owners (non-spouses) each get $250K on their share per Reg §1.121-2(a)(2). SMLLC treated as individually owned. Multi-member LLC generally cannot qualify (LLC is the "taxpayer"). Form 1099-S issuance threshold - if gross proceeds exceed $250K single / $500K MFJ. New Jersey notable non-conformity. California 3.33% gross withholding under Form 593 regardless of §121.
- section-754-743b-partnership-basis-adjustment.html: IRC §754 (Manner of electing optional adjustment to basis of partnership property) complete mechanics. Single irrevocable election covers BOTH §743(b) (transfer of partnership interest by sale, exchange, or death) AND §734(b) (distribution of partnership property to partner) - cannot elect separately. Election on timely-filed Form 1065 with attached statement; applies to year of triggering event AND all subsequent years. Irrevocable except with IRS consent under Reg §1.754-1(c) - rarely granted (acceptable grounds: change in nature of partnership business, substantial increase in assets, increased frequency of retirements/transfers). Late election relief via Reg §1.9100-2 (automatic 12-month) or §1.9100-3 (discretionary via PLR). §743(b) transferee partner adjustment - inside basis aligned with outside basis with respect to transferee only; computation = transferee's outside basis MINUS transferee's share of inside basis (per Reg §1.743-1(d) - share of previously taxed capital plus partnership liabilities). §734(b) adjustment to REMAINING partnership property upon distribution causing gain recognition or §732 basis adjustment - affects all remaining partners. TCJA P.L. 115-97 §13504 (December 22, 2017) added §743(d) MANDATORY substantial built-in loss adjustment - even without §754 election - if (1) partnership's adjusted basis exceeds FMV by more than $250,000 OR (2) transferee would be allocated loss exceeding $250,000 (Test 2 added by TCJA effective January 1, 2018). §734(d) mandatory §734(b) adjustment when substantial basis reduction (>$250K) exists. §708(b)(1)(B) technical termination REPEALED by TCJA - no longer triggers §743(b) refresh. §755 allocation among partnership properties: capital gain class vs ordinary income class first; within class by FMV. §197 intangibles (goodwill) - transferee's §743(b) adjustment amortized over 15 YEARS regardless of partnership's remaining recovery period (Reg §1.743-1(j)(4)(i)(B)). Reg §1.6011-18 finalized January 10, 2025 in T.D. 10024 - identifies four related-party basis adjustment transactions as transactions of interest; Form 8886 disclosure required when basis increases exceed gain recognized by $5,000,000; §6707A penalty up to $200,000 for failure to disclose. Rev. Rul. 2024-14 - IRS economic substance challenge to related-party basis transactions. Otay Project LP v. Commissioner, T.C. Memo 2026-21 (February 2026) - denied $714 million §743(b) deduction on economic substance grounds applying Ninth Circuit Sochin/Reddam framework. §1014 step-up at death coordination - §754 election allows §743(b) to bring inside basis up to stepped-up outside basis for heir; without election, heir bears phantom gain. §751(a) hot assets (unrealized receivables, substantially appreciated inventory) treated as IRD - NO step-up. Multi-tier partnerships - Reg §1.743-1(g) special tracking. K-1 Box 13 reporting; Reg §1.743-1(k)(2) - transferee must notify partnership within 30 days of transfer.
- cost-basis-step-up-1014-death.html: IRC §1014 (Basis of property acquired from a decedent) complete mechanics. §1014(a)(1) - basis equals FMV at date of decedent's death. §1014(a)(2) - alternate valuation date 6 months after death (§2032 election - all-or-nothing for entire estate, only available when reduces both gross estate AND estate tax). §1014(a)(3) - §2032A special use valuation for farm/business real estate (2026 maximum reduction $1,460,000 per Rev. Proc. 2025-32). OBBBA P.L. 119-21 (July 4, 2025) made $15,000,000 basic exclusion PERMANENT (replacing 2026 sunset to ~$7M); §1014 framework preserved entirely. §1014(b) property acquired from decedent categories: bequest/devise/inheritance, revocable trust assets, general power of appointment, joint property, community property both halves under §1014(b)(6), catch-all gross estate inclusion §1014(b)(9), QTIP property at second death §1014(b)(10). §1014(c) IRD exception - NO step-up for income in respect of a decedent under §691: traditional IRAs, 401(k)s, 403(b)s, qualified retirement plans, non-qualified deferred annuity gain, deferred compensation, Series EE/I bond accrued interest, installment notes (gain portion §453B(c)), cash basis accounts receivable, royalty payments accrued before death. §691(c) deduction for estate tax attributable to IRD partially relieves double taxation. §1014(e) boomerang rule - NO step-up if appreciated property gifted to decedent within 1 year of death and reacquired by donor or donor's spouse. §1014(b)(6) community property double step-up - both halves step up at first spouse's death in CA, AZ, TX, WA, NV, NM, ID, LA, WI - major advantage over JTWROS in non-community states (only decedent's half steps up under §2040(b)). Quasi-community property (CA only). §1014(f) consistent basis reporting - heir's basis cannot exceed Form 706 reported value when inclusion increased estate tax liability; Reg §1.1014-10 final regulations T.D. 9991 (September 17, 2024), corrected by T.D. 10054 (March 19, 2026); Form 8971 with Schedule A required from Form 706 estates; due earlier of 30 days after Form 706 due date with extensions OR 30 days after actual filing; §6662(k) 20% accuracy penalty for inconsistent basis claim. Rev. Rul. 2023-2 (April 14, 2023) - irrevocable grantor trust assets NOT in gross estate receive NO §1014 step-up despite grantor status for income tax. SLAT excluded from donor's estate - NO step-up; consider substitution power to swap high-basis assets pre-death. QTIP marital trust - inclusion in survivor's gross estate at second death triggers second step-up. Bypass/credit shelter trust - stepped up at first death only, no second step-up. §1223(9) inherited property automatically long-term. 2026 LTCG brackets per Rev. Proc. 2025-32: 0% up to $49,450 single / $98,900 MFJ; 15% to $545,500 / $613,700; 20% above. NIIT §1411 - 3.8% if MAGI thresholds exceeded. §751(a) hot assets in partnerships - no step-up (IRD treatment). Cross-border: NRA decedent estate tax exemption only $60,000; §2801 transfer tax 40% on US recipient of gifts from covered expatriates under §877A (January 2025 final regs); Form 3520 reporting for foreign inheritance over $100,000.
- section-877a-expatriation-mark-to-market.html: IRC §877A (Tax responsibilities of expatriation - HEART Act 2008 P.L. 110-245 effective June 17 2008) mark-to-market exit tax. All property of covered expatriate treated as sold on day before expatriation date for FMV. Net gain reduced by exclusion: $890,000 (2025), $910,000 (2026). Covered expatriate THREE tests under §877A(g)(1): (1) $206,000 (2025) average annual net income tax for 5 years prior; (2) $2,000,000 net worth at expatriation; (3) failure to certify 5-year tax compliance. Exceptions: dual citizens at birth, minors under 18½. §877A(c) exceptions: deferred compensation, specified tax-deferred accounts (IRAs/401(k)s treated as fully distributed at expatriation), nongrantor trusts (30% trustee withholding). §877A(d)(1) eligible deferred comp 30% payor withholding; §877A(d)(2) ineligible deferred comp present value treated as received. §877A(b) deferral election with adequate security bond and irrevocable treaty waiver; interest accrues. §877A(a)(3) wash sale §1091 NOT applied; §469 passive losses NOT triggered; §121 home sale NOT available for deemed sale. §2801 transfer tax 40% on covered gifts/bequests received by US person from covered expatriate - paid by US RECIPIENT not expatriate; final regulations T.D. 10018 (January 14 2025); Reg §28.2801-1 through §28.2801-7; Form 708 (Return of Tax for Gifts and Bequests Received From Covered Expatriates); annual exclusion under §2503(b) applies. Form 8854 (Initial and Annual Expatriation Information Statement) required - Initial in year of expatriation attached to final Form 1040/1040-NR; Annual if deferral or deferred comp continues. $10,000 penalty per year for Form 8854 failure. Long-term resident §877(e) 8 of 15 years; §7701(b)(6) LPR. §684 outbound trust transfer gain recognition. 2019 Relief Procedures for Certain Former Citizens for low-income/low-net-worth taxpayers.
- section-1245-1250-depreciation-recapture.html: IRC §1245 (Gain from dispositions of certain depreciable property - ordinary income recapture for personal property and specified real per §1245(a)(3)) and §1250 (Gain from dispositions of certain depreciable realty). §1245 recapture: ALL depreciation/amortization recaptured as ordinary income up to gain; covers tangible personal property, §197 intangibles, real property with §179/§168/§190/§169 deductions, bulk storage, single-purpose agricultural structures, NEW §168(n) qualified production property (OBBBA P.L. 119-21 added; placed in service after July 4 2025; designated by taxpayer). §1250 property: depreciable real property not §1245 - buildings, structural components. §1250(b) "additional depreciation" = excess of actual over straight-line; usually ZERO post-1986 because MACRS mandatory straight-line. Unrecaptured §1250 gain under §1(h)(1)(D) - portion of gain attributable to straight-line depreciation taxed at MAX 25% rate for individuals. OBBBA Notice 2026-11 (January 2026) confirmed 100% bonus depreciation PERMANENT under §168(k) for qualified property acquired after January 19 2025; pre-1/20/2025 acquisitions under TCJA phase-down (80%/60%/40%); component election for self-constructed property; §168(k)(10) reduced first-year rate election. §179 expensing 2026 max $2,560,000 with $4,090,000 phase-out (Rev. Proc. 2025-32); §179 deduction CONVERTS qualified real property (HVAC, roof, fire protection, security systems) to §1245 property to extent expensed - full ordinary recapture vs 25% unrecaptured §1250. §1031 like-kind exchange preserves recapture in replacement property basis. §453 installment: §1245 recognized IN FULL in year of sale per §453(i)(2); §1250 ratably. §291 C-corps additional 20% recapture on §1250 (effectively converts to §1245-style for corporations). §199A QBI deduction: §1245 ordinary recapture IS qualified business income (eligible for 20% §199A deduction - OBBBA P.L. 119-21 §70105 RETAINED 20% rate, did not increase to 23% as proposed in House version; OBBBA expanded phase-in ranges $50K→$75K single / $100K→$150K MFJ and added $400 minimum deduction); §1250 unrecaptured gain NOT QBI per §199A(c)(3)(B). §1014 step-up at death eliminates recapture (except IRD items §691). Form 4797 reporting Part III. §1400Z-2 Opportunity Zones - §1245/§1250 recapture NOT deferrable; only §1231 gain (after recapture) eligible for QOF.
- section-904-foreign-tax-credit-baskets.html: IRC §904 (Limitation on credit) foreign tax credit per-basket limitation. Formula: FTC LIMIT = US tax × (foreign source taxable income / worldwide taxable income), applied SEPARATELY per basket. SEVEN baskets post-TCJA (P.L. 115-97 effective 2018): (1) Passive §904(d)(2)(B); (2) General §904(d)(2)(D); (3) NCTI §951A category - RENAMED from GILTI by OBBBA P.L. 119-21 effective tax years after December 31 2025; (4) Foreign Branch §904(d)(2)(J); (5) §901(j) sanctioned country income; (6) Treaty-resourced §904(d)(6); (7) Lump-sum distributions §402(e). OBBBA changes effective 2026: §951A renamed NCTI (Net CFC Tested Income); QBAI 10% return on tangible assets ELIMINATED - all CFC tested income subject to NCTI; §250 deduction reduced - NCTI 50%→40% (effective tax rate ~14%), FDDEI 37.5%→33.34% (effective rate ~14%); §960 deemed-paid foreign tax credit on NCTI from 80% to 90% (haircut from 20% to 10%); §954(c)(6) CFC-to-CFC look-through made PERMANENT; §958(b)(4) downward attribution REINSTATED with anti-abuse rules (narrows incidental CFC classifications); new §951B added; §70311(a) expense allocation - interest and R&E NOT allocated to NCTI; SG&A only if "properly allocable"; 50% inventory foreign sourcing allowed for US-produced inventory sold abroad through foreign office. §904(c) carryover: 1-year back + 10-year forward per basket EXCEPT NCTI has NO carryover. §904(d)(3) look-through rules - CFC distributions categorized based on UNDERLYING CFC income character. High-tax kick-out §904(d)(2)(B)(iii)(II) - passive category income with foreign rate exceeding highest US rate reclassified to general basket. §954(b)(4) CFC high-tax exception election - 90% of US corporate rate (18.9% for 2026). §901(j) non-creditable taxes - sanctioned countries (Iran, North Korea, Sudan, Syria). 10% FTC haircut on §951A PTEP distributions on/after June 28 2025. Form 1116 individuals (one per basket); Form 1118 corporations. De minimis $300 single / $600 MFJ exception. Annual election to credit vs deduct. Reg §1.861-8 through §1.861-14 expense allocation. Reg §1.904-4 (high-tax kick-out); Reg §1.904-5 (look-through); Reg §1.904-6 (CFC tax allocation).
- form-3520a-foreign-grantor-trust.html: Form 3520-A (Annual Information Return of Foreign Trust With a US Owner) under IRC §6048(b) - filed BY the foreign trust (not the US owner). Required when any US person is treated as owner of any portion of foreign trust under grantor trust rules §§671-679 (excluding §678 for §6048 purposes). Due 15th day of 3rd MONTH after trust's tax year-end (March 15 for calendar-year trust; due March 15 2026 for tax year 2025). Form 7004 grants 6-month automatic extension to September 15; MUST use foreign trust's EIN not US owner's SSN/ITIN. SUBSTITUTE Form 3520-A required when foreign trustee fails to timely file - US owner completes substitute to best of ability, signs personally, attaches to own Form 3520 by Form 3520 deadline (April 15 or October 15 with extension); protects from §6677 penalty. Form 3520-A INCLUDES: Part I general info; Part II income statement; Part III balance sheet; Part IV Form 8938 anti-duplication checkbox; Foreign Grantor Trust Owner Statement (pages 3-4) provided to each US owner so they can report trust income on own Form 1040; Foreign Grantor Trust Beneficiary Statement (page 5) provided to each US beneficiary receiving distribution. ONLY COMPLETE form treated as timely filed - missing statements means treated as not filed. §6677 penalties: Form 3520-A failure greater of $10,000 OR 5% of trust value attributable to US owner; Form 3520 transfer/distribution failure greater of $10,000 OR 35%; large foreign gift failure 5% per month max 25%; additional $10,000 per 30 days after 90-day notice up to 100% of reportable amount; reasonable cause exception §6677(d) - foreign advisor reliance does NOT qualify. §679 grantor trust mechanics: §679(a)(1) US person transferring property to foreign trust with US beneficiary treated as owner; §679(d) presumption of US beneficiary; §679(a)(4) 5-YEAR look-back for pre-immigration trust contributions (new resident treated as transferring on residency start date); §679(a)(2)(B) FMV exception; Reg §1.679-4(d) qualified obligation. HIRE Act 2010 §533: §643(i) uncompensated use of trust property by US person treated as distribution at FMV; §643(i)(2) loans of cash/marketable securities to US person at non-market interest treated as distribution. §684 outbound transfer to foreign nongrantor trust requires immediate gain recognition. Rev. Proc. 2014-55 (Canadian RRSPs/RRIFs exempt from Forms 3520/3520-A); Rev. Proc. 2020-17 (tax-favored foreign retirement/savings trusts for medical/disability/educational benefits exempt for eligible individuals - exemption does NOT extend to FBAR or Form 8938). T.D. 10037 (January 2025) finalized proposed §6048 regulations. Schedule B Line 8 disclosure required on Form 1040.
- penalty-stacking-6651-6662-6663.html: IRC §6651 (Failure to file tax return or to pay tax) and §6662 (Imposition of accuracy-related penalty on underpayments) and §6663 (Imposition of fraud penalty - 75%) complete mechanics and stacking rules. §6651(a)(1) FTF 5% per month max 25%; minimum penalty lesser of $510 (2026 per Rev. Proc. 2025-32) or 100% of tax if more than 60 days late. §6651(a)(2) FTP 0.5% per month max 25%; §6651(c)(1) reduction when both apply (5% - 0.5% = 4.5% combined; max ~47.5%). §6651(d) FTP increase to 1.0%/month after §6331 notice of intent to levy. §6651(h) FTP reduced to 0.25%/month with valid installment agreement for timely filer. §6651(f) fraudulent FTF 15%/month max 75% - Counsel and managerial approval required. §6662 accuracy-related 20% penalty categories: §6662(b)(1) negligence/disregard; §6662(b)(2) substantial understatement (>10% or $5,000); §6662(b)(3) substantial valuation misstatement (150% threshold); §6662(b)(5) substantial estate/gift undervaluation (65% threshold); §6662(b)(6) economic substance (strict liability if undisclosed); §6662(b)(7) undisclosed foreign asset understatement. §6662(h) 40% gross misstatement; §6662(j) 40% undisclosed foreign asset. §6663 civil fraud 75% - IRS clear and convincing burden; §6663(b) presumption entire underpayment fraud-related; §6663(c) innocent spouse protection. Stacking: §6651 + §6662 stack (procedural + substantive); §6662 + §6663 CANNOT stack on same portion; §6651(f) + §6663 CAN stack. §6664(c) reasonable cause and good faith defense for §6662 (NOT §6662(b)(6) economic substance); §6651 uses "reasonable cause not willful neglect" - separate standard. First-Time Abate (FTA) per IRM 20.1.1.3.6.1 covers §6651(a)(1), §6651(a)(2), §6656 only - clean 3-year history required. §6751(b) supervisory approval written before personal proposal - except §6651, §6654, §6655, automatic e-calc. US v. Boyle 469 U.S. 241 (1985) - no advisor reliance defense for filing deadlines. Clay v. Commissioner 152 T.C. No. 13 (2019) - timing of supervisory approval. §6501 statute of limitations - 3 years general, 6 years for 25% omission, NO SOL for fraudulent returns under §6501(c)(1). Form 843 abatement procedure; supporting documentation; reasonable cause statement under penalty of perjury. Coordination with §6654/§6655 estimated tax, §6656 failure to deposit, §6676 erroneous refund claim, §6707A reportable transaction, §6694/§6695 preparer, §6677 foreign trust, §6038/§6038A/§6038C information returns.
- section-645-revocable-trust-as-estate.html: IRC §645 (Certain revocable trusts treated as part of estate) election complete mechanics. Qualified Revocable Trust (QRT) = trust treated as owned by decedent under §676 (revocable trust) on date of death per §645(b)(1). Election permits QRT and decedent's estate to file COMBINED Form 1041 during "election period." Form 8855 (Election to Treat a Qualified Revocable Trust as Part of an Estate) jointly signed by executor and trustee; attached to first combined Form 1041 by due date including extensions; IRREVOCABLE once made. Election period §645(b)(2): begins on date of death; "applicable date" ends 2 years post-death if no Form 706 required, OR LATER of 2 years/6 months after final 706 determination if 706 required. Benefits: FISCAL YEAR election (any month-end up to 12 months post-death; tax deferral and income-shifting; estates only); §642(c) charitable deduction expansion (amounts paid OR permanently set aside vs trust limited to "paid"); §6654(l) 2-YEAR estimated tax exemption (no quarterly payments first 2 years post-death; trust loses this if no election); §469(i) $25,000 special allowance active rental real estate available; income consolidation simplifies fiduciary accounting; S-corp eligibility under §1361(c)(2)(A)(iii) as estate (continues throughout election period vs trust limited to 2 years from death under §1361(c)(2)(A)(ii) ESBT/QSST). End of election period §645(g): QRT treated as receiving distribution from estate; basis preserved (no second §1014 step-up); NOL and §469 PAL carryovers transfer to QRT; final combined return marked "Final Return"; §642(h) excess deductions flow to beneficiaries one-time. Multiple QRTs each require separate Form 8855. Reg §1.645-1 (T.D. 8959 - 2001); Treasury Decision 9982 (June 2024) anti-abuse rules. Reg §301.9100-1/-3 late election relief discretionary (PLR required). Coordination with foreign QRT (Forms 3520/3520-A), pour-over wills, 65-day rule §663(b), §642(h) excess deductions at termination, state conformity issues (California decoupled).
- section-382-nol-limitation-ownership-change.html: IRC §382 (Limitation on net operating loss carryforwards and certain built-in losses following ownership change) complete mechanics. Loss corporation §382(k)(1) - corp with NOL carryforward, NOL for year of ownership change, or net unrealized built-in loss; TCJA §13301 (P.L. 115-97 December 22, 2017) expanded to include §163(j) disallowed business interest carryforwards. Ownership change §382(g)(1) - increase of MORE THAN 50 percentage points in stock held by 1+ 5-percent shareholders during 3-year testing period ending on owner shift or equity structure shift. Owner shift §382(g)(2) - any stock ownership change affecting 5-percent shareholder. Equity structure shift §382(g)(3) - tax-free reorganization under §368(a)(1)(A)/(B)/(C)/(D)/(F)/(G) (excluding divisive and mere recapitalization). 5-percent shareholder §382(k)(7) - direct or indirect owner of 5% or more; non-5% aggregated as single "public group" per §382(g)(4)(A). §382 LIMITATION §382(b) = VALUE of loss corporation immediately before ownership change × LONG-TERM TAX-EXEMPT RATE (published monthly by IRS; ~4-4.5% range 2026; locked at change date). §382(b)(2) unused limitation carries forward and adds to future year limits. §382(l)(1) anti-stuffing - capital contributions in 2 years before change disallowed if intended to inflate limit. NUBIG/NUBIL §382(h) - threshold LESSER of $10M or 15% of asset FMV; 5-year recognition period. NUBIG INCREASES §382 limit when recognized; NUBIL treats recognized loss as pre-change loss subject to limitation. Notice 2003-65 - safe harbor methods for NUBIG/NUBIL: 338 approach, 1374 approach, hybrid. §382(c) Continuity of Business Enterprise - loss corp must continue historic business OR use significant historic assets in business for 2 YEARS post-change; failure = TOTAL DISALLOWANCE of pre-change losses (not just limitation). §382(l)(3) modifies §318 attribution (family limited; partnership/estate simplified; option deemed-exercise rules). §382(l)(5) bankruptcy exception - alternative to standard §382 with 50% NOL reduction for post-petition interest; subsequent ownership change within 2 years wipes out NOLs entirely. §382(l)(6) alternative bankruptcy stepped-up value rule. §383 applies similar limitations to general business credit, AMT credit, foreign tax credit carryforwards. Reg §1.382-1 through §1.382-12. Tax Reform Act of 1986 created modern §382. TCJA P.L. 115-97 §13301 expanded scope. CARES Act 2020 temporary modifications (expired). OBBBA P.L. 119-21 did NOT modify §382 core mechanics. Coordination with §269 (acquisitions to evade tax - hard disallowance), §338(h)(10) and §336(e) deemed asset sales, consolidated return SRLY rules.
- section-1256-contracts-mark-to-market.html: IRC §1256 (Section 1256 contracts marked to market) complete mechanics. Five contract categories §1256(b)(1): (A) regulated futures contracts (CME, ICE, NYMEX, CBOT - ES, CL, GC, ZB, ZC, etc.); (B) foreign currency contracts (interbank market settling within 2 business days on currencies with regulated futures trading - major pairs USD/EUR, USD/JPY, etc.); (C) nonequity options (broad-based index options SPX/NDX/RUT/VIX, options on commodity futures, currency futures, Treasury futures, debt instruments); (D) dealer equity options (listed equity options held by registered options dealers); (E) dealer securities futures contracts. EXCLUSIONS §1256(b)(2): single-stock futures (non-dealer); interest rate, currency, basis, commodity, equity, equity index, credit default swaps; equity options not held by dealer. Broad-based index per Reg §1.1256(b)-1: 10+ component securities, no >30% weighting, top 5 <60%. SPX qualifies; SPY (ETF) does NOT. Mark-to-market §1256(a): each contract held at close of taxable year treated as SOLD for FMV on last business day; gain/loss recognized currently; basis adjusted to FMV. 60/40 RULE §1256(a)(3): regardless of holding period, gain/loss = 60% long-term capital + 40% short-term capital. Maximum 2026 federal rate ~26.8% (60% × 20% LTCG + 40% × 37% ordinary; plus 3.8% NIIT). Loss carryback §1256(c): 3-YEAR carryback election against prior §1256 gains; carryforward indefinitely if unused; Form 1045 fast-track (12-month deadline) vs Form 1040X. Wash sale §1091 NOT APPLIED §1256(f). Hedge exception §1256(e): identified hedging transactions opt out of §1256 - ordinary gain/loss treatment matching hedged item; Reg §1.1221-2(f) identification required on day acquired. Mixed straddle elections §1256(d)(1) (identified mixed straddle) and §1256(d)(2) (mixed straddle account). §1256(a)(4) special rule for dealer equity options of limited partners/limited entrepreneurs - all short-term (no 60/40). §988 foreign currency coordination - spot forex default §988 ordinary; §988(a)(1)(B) election out to capital (NOT §1256); §1256(g)(2) forex futures automatic §1256. Form 6781 reporting (Gains and Losses From Section 1256 Contracts and Straddles); Part I §1256 contracts flowing to Schedule D Parts I and II. 1099-B Box 11 aggregate profit/loss including year-end MTM. State conformity - most states tax all capital gains as ordinary; 60/40 federal benefit doesn't transfer (exceptions: Arizona, Montana, New Mexico preferential LTCG). Tax Reform Act of 1981 P.L. 97-34 created §1256. Deficit Reduction Act of 1984 P.L. 98-369 renamed from "regulated futures contracts." Dodd-Frank Act P.L. 111-203 (2010) excluded swaps. OBBBA P.L. 119-21 did NOT modify §1256. §263(g) carrying cost capitalization for non-§1256 straddle positions; §1256-only positions exempt. §475(f) trader election - separate from §1256; commodity trader election generally loses 60/40 benefit.
- section-1259-constructive-sale-appreciated-position.html: IRC §1259 (Constructive sales treatment for appreciated financial positions) complete mechanics. Requires gain recognition when a taxpayer holding an appreciated financial position (AFP) enters a transaction that substantially eliminates BOTH the risk of loss AND opportunity for gain. AFP §1259(b)(1) - any position in stock, debt instrument, or partnership interest with built-in gain (FMV exceeds basis). Constructive sale triggers §1259(c)(1): (A) short sale of same or substantially identical property (short against the box); (B) offsetting notional principal contract (total return equity swap); (C) futures or forward contract to deliver; (D) acquisition where AFP is itself a short/NPC/futures position; (E) other transactions per regulations. Consequences §1259(a): (1) gain recognized as if AFP sold at FMV on constructive sale date; (2) basis increased by gain recognized; (3) new holding period begins. Only GAIN recognized (AFP by definition has built-in gain); losses never recognized. Character capital, short or long-term per AFP holding period at constructive sale date. Closed transaction exception §1259(c)(3): NOT a constructive sale if (i) offsetting transaction closed before end of 30th day after close of taxable year; (ii) taxpayer holds AFP throughout 60-day period beginning when transaction closed; AND (iii) at no time during that 60-day period is risk of loss diminished by other positions. Related person attribution §1259(c)(4) - §267(b)/§707(b). Variable prepaid forward contract (VPFC) avoids constructive sale by retaining meaningful upside (variable share delivery); Rev. Rul. 2003-7 (properly structured VPFC not constructive sale); Anschutz Co. v. Commissioner 135 T.C. 78 (2010) aff'd 10th Cir. (VPFC combined with share lending was current sale). Coordination with §1091 wash sale, §1233 short sales, §1256 MTM, §1411 NIIT, §1014 step-up (lost if constructive sale during life). §7701(o) economic substance for monetization. Enacted by Taxpayer Relief Act of 1997 P.L. 105-34 to eliminate short-against-the-box deferral. Form 8949 / Schedule D reporting.
- section-6166-estate-tax-installment-closely-held.html: IRC §6166 (Extension of time for payment of estate tax where estate consists largely of interest in closely held business) complete mechanics. Lets an executor elect to pay estate tax attributable to a closely held business interest in installments over up to 14 years, preventing forced sale of family businesses. Qualification §6166(a)(1): value of closely held business interest in gross estate must EXCEED 35% of adjusted gross estate (AGE = gross estate minus §2053 funeral/administration/debt deductions and §2054 casualty/theft losses). Decedent must be US citizen or resident. Closely held business §6166(b)(1): (A) sole proprietorship; (B) partnership where 20%+ capital interest in estate OR 45 or fewer partners; (C) corporation where 20%+ voting stock value in estate OR 45 or fewer shareholders. Payment structure §6166(a): up to 10 equal annual installments; first principal installment due no later than 5 years after original estate tax due date; first 5 years may be interest-only; total deferral up to 14 years; interest payable annually throughout. 2-percent portion §6166(b)(2): estate tax attributable to first $1,890,000 (2026 inflation-adjusted from $1,000,000 base since 1998; verify current Rev. Proc.) of taxable business value above applicable exclusion bears 2% interest; tax above the 2% portion bears interest at 45% of the §6601 underpayment rate per §6601(j). Interest NOT deductible for income or estate tax §2053(c)(1)(D). Aggregation §6166(c) - two or more businesses treated as one if 20%+ of each in estate. Passive assets §6166(b)(9) excluded from business value. Holding company stock election §6166(b)(8). Acceleration §6166(g): disposition or withdrawal of 50%+ of business interest, failure to pay any installment within 6 months, or undistributed net income not applied makes all unpaid tax due. §6324A special estate tax lien in lieu of bond. §303 stock redemption to pay death taxes coordinates without triggering acceleration. §2032A special use valuation coordination. Graegin loan alternative if §6166 unavailable. OBBBA $15M permanent exemption reduces estates needing §6166 but large family businesses still benefit. Form 706 timely election.
- section-367-outbound-transfer-foreign-corporation.html: IRC §367 (Foreign corporations) complete mechanics. Overrides normal tax-free treatment of corporate formations, reorganizations, and liquidations when a US person transfers property to a FOREIGN corporation - treats the foreign corp as not a corporation so gain is recognized; prevents untaxed migration of appreciated assets and intangibles offshore. §367(a)(1) general rule: US person transferring property to foreign corp in §332/§351/§354/§356/§361 exchange recognizes GAIN (losses not recognized, computed item-by-item). TCJA P.L. 115-97 §14102 REPEALED the active foreign trade or business exception for transfers after December 31, 2017 - most outbound tangible property now triggers gain. Stock transfers: US transferor who is 5%+ shareholder of transferee foreign corp may defer gain via 5-year Gain Recognition Agreement (GRA) under Reg §1.367(a)-8; annual certification required; triggering events §1.367(a)-8(j) (disposition of transferred stock or substantially all assets) cause retroactive gain plus interest; reasonable cause relief Reg §1.367(a)-8(p); Form 8838 statute extension. §367(d) intangibles: outbound transfer of intangible property (§367(d)(4) definition - patents, copyrights, goodwill, going concern value, workforce in place, customer lists, know-how) treated as sale for CONTINGENT payments; US transferor includes annual income commensurate with income over useful life (super-royalty; §482 coordination); 20-year default for indefinite-life intangibles. TCJA EXPANDED §367(d)(4) to include goodwill, going concern value, workforce in place - closed the prior foreign goodwill exception. Final regs T.D. 10009 (October 9, 2024) address repatriation of intangibles back to US person, terminating §367(d) inclusions; §904 FTC coordination. §367(b) inbound and foreign-to-foreign transactions; all earnings and profits amount inclusion; §1248 and PTEP coordination. §367(e) §355 distributions and §332 liquidations to foreign persons. Form 926 reporting under §6038B - penalty 10% of FMV (max $100,000 unless intentional disregard); §6501(c)(8) keeps statute of limitations open on entire return until missing information filed. Cost sharing arrangement under §1.482-7 not a §367 transfer. Branch loss recapture §367(a)(3)(C).
- section-83b-election-restricted-stock.html: IRC §83(b) (election to include in gross income in year of transfer) complete mechanics. Default §83(a): property transferred for services subject to substantial risk of forfeiture taxed as ordinary income when it VESTS, at then-current FMV minus amount paid. §83(b) election lets the service provider instead include the excess of FMV over amount paid at the time of TRANSFER (grant) - locking in a low or zero taxable amount and converting all future appreciation into long-term capital gain on sale rather than ordinary income at vesting. STRICT 30-day deadline §83(b)(2): election must be filed with the IRS within 30 days of the property transfer (restricted stock grant, or option early-exercise date); no extensions, no §301.9100 relief, one day late is fatal. Applies to restricted stock awards (RSAs) and shares acquired by EARLY EXERCISE of options (ISO or NSO, 30 days from exercise) and founder stock; does NOT apply to RSUs (unfunded promise, no property transferred at grant) or unexercised options. Holding period §83(f) begins at grant for electors (vs at vesting otherwise) - critical for 1-year LTCG and §1202 QSBS 5-year holding requirement. ISO coordination - early exercise plus §83(b) at minimal spread (FMV minus strike) reduces §56(b)(3) AMT preference. Forfeiture risk §83(b)(1): if property forfeited after election, NO deduction for income previously recognized (only capital loss for amount paid). Irrevocable except IRS consent for mistake of fact. Employer §83(h) deduction matches amount included in service provider income. Employee §83(b) income subject to FICA and income tax withholding at grant. Must furnish copy to employer/service recipient. Form 15620 standardized IRS election template released November 2024 (optional; compliant written statement still acceptable). Profits interest protective election (Rev. Proc. 93-27 / 2001-43). §409A coordination for below-FMV NSO. §83(i) qualified equity grant 5-year deferral (separate election). State conformity varies.
